title: Between Tradition and Family Violence: Exploring Sociocultural Barriers to Reporting Child-to-Parent Abuse in Nigeria
abstract: Despite the increasing global attention into child-to-parent violence (CPV), studies examining the influence of sociocultural and religious beliefs on parental coping and reporting behaviors, especially in conservative societies, are scarce. This study explores the victimological experiences of Nigerian parents on how cultural norms shaped their recognition, coping strategies, and reporting of CPV. The study recruited 17 women who had experienced CPV through women's advocacy groups and family services units. Semi-structured interviews sought to understand how cultural values, norms, and beliefs affect parents' recognition, response, and reporting of CPV. A thematic analysis of narratives was carried out. The study found patriarchy, family hierarchies and honor, gender roles, social expectations, religious values, and spiritual beliefs as potent sociocultural factors that shaped parents' reactions to CPV. Unlike other forms of family violence, reporting CPV hinges on deeper cultural and religious underpinnings with severe social consequences. The findings have important practical and policy implications.
authors_text: Aborisade, Richard A.; Oladejo, Segilola Y.
